---
title: "Frontline Treatment of Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A Systematic Review (2016-2026)"
subtitle: "A Systematic Literature Review"
date: "2026-03-26"
author: "Automated Literature Review Pipeline"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    cite-method: citeproc
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.5
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{prisma-flow-diagram/prisma-flow-diagram}
  docx:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/american-medical-association.csl
abstract: |
  **Background:** Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is the most common subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, accounting for approximately 30--40% of new diagnoses worldwide. For over two decades, rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) has served as the standard frontline regimen, curing roughly 60% of patients. However, the remaining 40% experience primary refractory disease or relapse, underscoring a critical unmet need. The period from 2016 to 2026 has witnessed transformative advances in molecular classification, antibody--drug conjugates, bispecific antibodies, and chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapies that are reshaping the frontline treatment landscape.
  
  **Methods:** A systematic review was conducted following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines. Comprehensive searches of Scopus, PubMed, and Embase were performed for English-language articles published between January 2016 and March 2026. The initial search yielded 500 records. After screening by title and abstract, followed by full-text eligibility assessment applying predefined inclusion criteria---peer-reviewed original research, clinical trials, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses published in journals with a CiteScore of 3.0 or higher---50 articles across 26 journals were included in the qualitative synthesis.
  
  **Results:** The included studies demonstrated that R-CHOP remains the backbone of frontline therapy, although the POLARIX trial established polatuzumab vedotin plus R-CHP (pola-R-CHP) as a new standard with superior progression-free survival. Molecular subtyping based on cell of origin (activated B-cell--like versus germinal center B-cell--like) and genetic classifiers has refined risk stratification, yet subtype-directed strategies such as ibrutinib plus R-CHOP and lenalidomide plus R-CHOP have not yielded consistent overall survival benefits. CAR-T cell therapy has demonstrated curative potential in the relapsed and refractory setting and is under investigation as frontline consolidation for high-risk patients. Bispecific antibodies and novel antibody--drug conjugates are expanding the therapeutic arsenal. Prognostic indices including the International Prognostic Index and its revised variants, along with double-hit and double-expresser biology, remain central to treatment decision-making.
  
  **Conclusions:** The frontline treatment of DLBCL is undergoing a paradigm shift driven by molecularly informed risk stratification and the emergence of targeted immunotherapies. While pola-R-CHP represents an incremental advance over R-CHOP, curative outcomes for high-risk molecular subtypes remain elusive. Future research should prioritize biomarker-guided treatment selection, earlier integration of CAR-T and bispecific antibody platforms, and strategies to overcome resistance in genetically adverse subgroups. Collaborative, molecularly stratified clinical trials are essential to translate these advances into durable survival improvements across all patient subsets.
---

{{< include sections/01-introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/02-methods.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/03-pathogenesis.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/04-diagnosis.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/05-etiology.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/06-treatment.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/07-covid.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/08-discussion.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/09-prisma-checklist.qmd >}}

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
